text: Appear to us, Protector-Bull.
Appear to us, Protector-Bull.
White one, Proclaims-the-herd-from-afar,
Little scourge of fearful rulers,
You are a child of Igalami and Bashaija,
Of the Raiser-of-Howls in early morning,
Climber-of-men Giant leopard of Kahigi,
My lord, Protector-Bull,
When I lay down to sleep I dreamed,
Awake I started with a big head roaring.
"My lord may this big head burst!
This head be bound with stones!
This head be bound with kaihyula herb!"
In our house I'd recently been married.
Inside I have a new bride.
[ Appear! Child of Bifu fish of Kitahya.
Bellowers-that-keep-silent lake Ikimba is your home.
Child of the Bugabo road.
I place before you a throne to sit. ]
Her name is Beloved-one of Bunyana.
She-makes-a-man-feel-shame is certain that she's loved.
In just the way those women interrupt -
The little woman broke my dream.
What knuckles I tapped her head with!
I hear the little woman soothe me,
"Why chastise me love?
Never have I said an angry word to you.
Nor since the dawn last lightened have we fought."
I say, "I'm hitting you and justly so
Because you broke my dream."
She said, "I was not thinking it a dream."
I was driving off the things that made you moan."
I said, "If you were Nkwanzi Lutane
Or (my other wife) Mabuye of Ntale,
I'd use the edge of a hafted-knife,
And come next day, wife,
Your father and your mother would arbitrate the pieces."
Elder women know.
They arose from sleep prepared.
They said, "What was your dream, spirit-medium?"
I said, "What I dreamed today, tomorrow after dawn, I will prophesy to the Muzinza.
O Protector-Bull!
He is the White-one, Proclaims-the-herd-from-afar,
Little scourge of fearful rulers,
Appear! Great-tongued one, Breaker-of-termite mounds,
Little switch winds lines on kings' behinds.
He is Lyangombe of Kabale.
Appear! Seed-bull of the Bankango."
"My lord, what was your dream, spirit medium?"
I said, "What I dreamed today I will prophesy to the Muzinza."
Those women did not delay that day.
Pot they put on the flame.
Pith from a banana plant they used to cleanse me.
Spear they put on sharpening block.
I saw my hafted-knife they polish.
They did not ask another word, the women.
My calabash they cleaned out with stones.
A bit of beer
My lord, they poured enough to sip and think with.
Beer to sip, relax and think with.
With butterfat they rubbed my shins,
A bit, not much, the Water-of-the-cattle-herdsmen.
They did not ask another word.
I put on a spotted otter fur,
A black otter sweeps the air.
I held my spear point-down in readiness.
Then I descended into the wilderness of lions.
I climbed the mountain of hyenas.
My lord, Provider
It was I in Ntoija.
I descended into Bukongo.
At Kyenyabasa's river shore
I cast my eyes on Bujuli the ferryman.
"What is it, spirit medium?
I see you wipe off sweat."
He said, "What I dreamed today, tomorrow
After dawn, I will prophesy to the Muzinza."
Bijuli Bijuli,
Bujuli of Kwenyunga,
"The-cause-of-envious-growls be healed!"
He answered, "Hurry, heal me and we'll see."
He put me in the canoe. The other shore!
I crossed to the mountain of leopards.
My lord, I did not ask another word.
It is I in Kanyina,
In Kanyinayo.
That day I climbed a mountain,
My lord, Provider
It is I, my lord, at Matembe,
Kihilila and Kyebyazi.
It is I in Kishenye of the munazi trees.
At Hoe-swallower field.
In Kigoye up above
I descended to the fence at Lwiza's in Bwala.
In Kangabushalo at Lwogo's
I crossed the water at Busano.
In Lwamulumba in the middle
I pushed on to the two trees at Kalabagaine.
O Protector-Bull!
In Nyanga-ya-Iyele.
In Byonyu-bishaija field.
Lo my eye is met by Abayondo clansmen.
"What is it spirit medium what is it?
We see you wipe off sweat."
He said, "In the king's presence I beg leave to offer a salutation."
They said, "The man's drunk beer in the light of dawn.
You seem intoxicated.
[Fellow, we pity you].
How will you reach him to salute?"
He said, "In the royal court, is there an illness?
Or is there an order that it be cleared?
Or is there a great misfortune?"
"The man's drunk beer in the light of dawn.
To me he seems intoxicated.
There is Gahulwa, Gahulwa,
The Binding-rope, Blind in one eye.
With his quarter-staff, Wraps-it-up-easily,
He cracks Bahyoza's backs.
He said, "He's beating them and justly so.
They bring his beating on themselves.
When I set down a calabash of beer,
Then add a roll of tobacco,
A festive packet, look, of coffee berries,
He'll free me to go and salute."
He did not ask another word.
He said, "Himself, in which house is he now?
Which house?"
They said, "That man! Look here,
There is the Straightener-who-never-fails ."
"How does he straighten, the king?
Does he chop folk with hafted-knives
Or does he ply them with medicines?
Or does he, perhaps, massage the body?
If you don't know the way he straightens -
The Owner-of-bulls straightens with property.
When he grants you a farm in Katoma,
'The birthplace of a family is land.'
When he grants you cattle and a farm,
They are, my lord, the spoils he took at Kabizimbo."
Oh Provider!
"We hear the Chew-you-up troop is making accusations.
The Mulalamba troop clutch his knees.
When we left, his strategy in mankala was 'stockpile'.
The king was playing 'cattle herd.'
He's leaned against Njwanga,
But his pupils seek Lutwiga.
Ilamulila 'bleeds cattle with a dart.'
When we left Lwela had 'tied the banana bast.'"
He said, " Conqueror-of-many and Kemukyalo
Are the ones with whom he plays the mankala.
My lord, Provider
My lord, Provider
And Katogoita Kaishamako
A Shield-breaker sent by Nkwele."
He did not ask another word.
He descended to Kigenda-kuzimu.
To Byonyu-bishaija he climbed.
Kaitaba and the Stinger troop
He saw him running them in competition.
Byanjweli, Trammeler-of-spearshafts ,
Was setting stockade posts.
He did not ask another word.
Oh Protector-Bull!
By the royal approach-road
My lord, he reached the guardhouse.
He met Gahulwa,
Gahulwa, The Binding-rope, Blind in one eye .
"Respectful greetings, Gahulwa,
Respectful greetings, Binding-rope of the Vanguard
He said, "My lord, I beg leave...
My lord, in the king's presence I beg leave to offer a salutation."
"Easy does it, fellow
Be assured that easy does it."
A calabash of beer,
A roll of tobacco,
A festive packet of coffee berries,
"Go quickly, salute the king, fellow.
Go quickly, salute the king.
I'm coming to the royal court to order it be cleared."
With his special garments on his back
It was he in the royal approach road.
I saw him enter the gate.
At the iron bars that chime a warning,
At the fire of the Brown-flecked Bull,
I saw him strike his leather cloak to sit.
"Live forever,
Live forever, Father creator.
Live forever, Great-one-before-whom-I-am-powerless.
I salute you with a greeting of fear,
I also place before you one to buy my life.
For the Bankango of Kabale are easily angered
Men are killed for nothing.
You might put out my eyes,
You might put out my eyes,
And also cut my legs -
See! In the royal approach-road, I shoo away the birds from drying crops.
When I lay down to sleep I dreamed,
Awake I started with a big head roaring.
"My lord may this big head burst!
My lord, this head be bound with stones!
My lord, this head be bound with kaihyula herb!
I dreamed of a man of Itoju, Stern-face,
A man of Itoju, Stern-face,
A man of Itoju, Stern-face,
Tse-tse fly that climbs a buffalo's face,
First fruit of Kalemera,
He burned a royal drum while still a youth.
I dreamed he camped on Cowrie Mountain
And came striding along to Kyabagenzi."
He said, "Easy does it, fellow.
You say what my serpent describes.
If this comes to pass today, tomorrow I
Will give you what your serpent wants.
Go to the Bayondo clansmen, they'll cook you plantains."
Plantains did they go and peel.
When the plantain pot made simmering sounds
We heard a cry from Kalabagaine.
He said, "What cry is this?"
What is this cry, Bayoza?
It is a cry that stops the heart."
They said, "The Chew-you-up troop went to hunt at dawn.
Perhaps they've killed an otter."
He said, "I'd be hearing ' Big-cheeks. '"
He said, "I'd be hearing the hunter's roar."
"Perhaps they've killed a waterbuck
He said, "I'd be hearing ' Rustling,'
I would be hearing, ' Pisser-in-the-marsh,'
That is, 'Worn-by-the-beautiful'
I would have heard 'Destroyed-by-the-ugly'
And 'Worn-by-the-beautiful.'"
A wailing cry, a wailing cry
A wailing cry came in the door.
One said, "They must have killed a waterbuck there,
Worn by the beautiful. "
The wailing cry did not leave off, the wailing cry...
"They might have killed a mongoose there."
I'd be hearing ' Apportioner-of-trails. '
I would be hearing ' Sounds-of-an-enanga. '"
"They might have killed an omunyima there."
I'd be hearing ' Lowing-like-a-mother-cow.'
I'd be hearing 'Anus-that-has-a-hoe-and-a-hafted-knife-inside' "
"They might have killed an olutoni there."
"I'd be hearing ' Spread-himself-with-spots,'
There'd be 'Smeared-himself-with-rank-odor.' "
When the wailing cry did not let up, did not let up...
When the wailing cry of Muneko, of the wilderness...
When he had come,
He said, "Where is Kitekele?"
He said, "Where is Kitekele?"
They said, "In the luchwamba grove. He's playing mankala."
They said, "In the luchwamba grove. He's playing mankala."
When he had cast his eyes on him,
He said, "If you'd have been Flows-over-borders,
If you'd have been Kabushumba Itimba,
Or your own Kaitaba,
Or Kagambi,
That mankala game I'd have thrown at you.
They are plundered, the Gentle-footed ones!
The Sorghum tassels , Provider.
I tell you this O King we see only their tufted tails.
Your favorite calf, the one to whom you gave the special pasture,
My lord, the one to whom you gave the special pasture at Kijongo,
They drove it away at the rear of the herd.
As it scampered off, its neck bells loosely jangled."
I saw this come upon the king as he was sipping, swallowing.
I saw them tilt the royal calabash back.
I heard him say, "What shall I do?"
He said, "The fellow has not plundered cattle!
What shall I do, my lord?
He's chopped apart the wooden vessels of our hearth!"
He said, "He camped on Cowrie Mountain
And came striding along to Kyabagenzi."
He said, "I tell you Bugengele of Nyawela"
He said, "I saw, proclaims itself in flames."
He said, "Musila of Luhungu"
He said, "as well, proclaims itself in flames."
He said, "Ngalama of Kabi"
He said, "my lord, proclaims itself in flames."
He said, "But in Kishogo of Lushu"
He said, "he honored (your steward) Eishamba."
He said, "Kishogo of Kayango
He left untouched, desiring it for his own.
Ntobola and Bikiga
My lord, those both proclaim themselves in flames.
Bugengele of Nyawela
As well, proclaims itself in flames."
He said, "What shall I do, my men?"
This came upon the king as he was sipping, swallowing.
But when he unblinked his eyes, see! They'd reddened.
He said, "What shall I do?" the king.
When he put out his chest,
You'd say maidens put out milk vessel caps.
When he showed his buttock,
You'd say it looked like a smooth, hard, sun-drying place.
When he stood, and backs of knees appeared,
You'd say a roof-peak dipped.
When he shook his hair, the king,
It looked like waving yojwe grass.
He said, "Call those doctors of mine."
They summoned Busy-are-they
They summoned Trial-maker and Abandoned-one.
He said, "Do that water oracle of mine."
They said, "Stir up the oracle of Kitulu.
It comes from Bwelanyange.
Speak, speak.
Speak the words of Little-doctor and of Charcoal,
And of Blood, Preventer-of-things-to-come .
Speak. Say, 'This is the way we shall raid.'"
When they consulted it, it answered yes.
They saw him burn Kihanja.
That day lo! He left it bare earth.
He said, "What can I do, child?
Sound the drums."
[Drums sounded in reverberating roar.]
I heard him call Never-gives-in of Byabato.
He said, "See that you bind up provisions.
And you, Didn't-I-tell-them-so Kitano,
And see that you bind up provisions.
And you Milk-vessel-cap of Speared-one,"
He said, "and you bind up provisions.
Lyakanazi and Lulongo,"
He said, "and see that you bind up provisions.
Child, Child Not-like-a-bad-debt,"
She answered, "My lord, The-eye-of-a-hateful-one."
He said, "Bind up charms on your waist. I go to war."
He summoned Mushumbusi Ndiita.
He summoned Kikanya Kagondo.
He said, "You as well, my men,"
He said, "Guard my things."
He said, "I go to war I cannot shrink from it."
I heard the drums roar with a will.
Self Praise Recitation Begins
I am the Impaler, the Wound-that-never-heals.
I attack lurching and reeling like One-full-of-wrath.
I climbed a tree
But have no fingers or claws.
Father was ill, and they said, 'You'll not inherit.'
That's why I came for directions to a healer.
They said, 'He's at Ntoija. I descended to Bukongo.
That's when I came to Crow.
Crow brought meat and gave it to me.
He took it from an open grave.
He said, 'Go, give it to your father, be he well.'
That's why I came.
When I came I spread it on him.
I am a wooden crook.
I am an adder.
I am Moves-nervously-watching.
I am She-who-feels-another's-pain of Makes-certain.
I went to Uganda.
They bent me over and found hair filling an anus.
I came and killed a Ganda.
The Ganda they dispose of.
They say, 'Where does that one come from?'
They say, 'He comes from Place-of-Journeyers.'
Shine forth Father creator .
These spears are yours .
Self-Praise Poetry ends
I heard the king as he reached his palace roadway, roadway, roadway,
He said, "I'll descend at Nzilonke.
I'll tread the house lane of the Bakulwa."
They said, "My lord, pass by Kyakahunga.
Go and reach the house lane of the Bakulwa.
When he entered the house lane of the Bakulwa at Kayanga, Kayanga,
Suddenly he glanced into a tree, into a mushasha tree.
Lo! He saw a monkey.
He said, "Child with blackest black lips,"
He said, "The Hinda are burning their plantains!"
He said, "Yo-yo-yo-yo," he said, "so, they've straightened their tails.
Like spearshafts, they've straightened them well!"
They said, "My lord, don't joke with a monkey."
He said, "And why should I not joke?
Among Hinda women I'm a grandchild."
Self Praise Recitation Begins
I am Foiler, child of Essential-warrior
Obliged-one, Bad-dream, child of Causer-of-retreats
An Ancient Feud.
You see I fought in far off lands.
I raided in Zinzaland, then raided in Bunyoro.
I killed a man there.
I raided in Buganda of Ntege at the home of Junju.
And at the home of They-don't-let-me-be I killed a man.
An ancient feud placed a scar on my eye.
I am of Bushalita and the Decider-of-cases,
What-holds-you-in-its-grasp.
Go regally, Father creator.
These spears are yours .
Self-Praise Poetry ends
I heard him summon Foiler child of Essential-warrior.
He said, "Do these unclean tasks."
"One's king does not despise him for them."
" Eyes Redden in Anger,
Little-scourge, I dwelled among Speakers-of-the-unspeakable,"
He said, "Go with the Mulalamba troop The-Routers, The-Routers.
Go with the Eaters-of-the-clash-of-battle troop.
Go with the Soft-Spoken Muziba.
Travel by the ford at Kyenyabasa."
He summoned Causer-of-retreats Obliged-one.
He said, "Go with the Mirth-makers troop,
I make him merry who made merry the Self-Invited. "
He said, "Go with the Eaters-of-the-clash-of-battle troop.
Go with the Shield-breakers,
Who ate the quail of Buyenje,
People of the Hima Tologo,
Men of Nkwele, who moves like waving swampgrass, "
Mulamula tree fears the boundaries,
Feeder of calves that wanted to return home and play.
He said, "Descend,
At Kalebe we descend upon them."
I heard a Muhamba.
"Hey Babwiko!"
"Hey Babwiko!"
"Did you hear?" "We heard the royal drums."
"Listen they're sounding."
Hey! Let them come. Our bows will make them piss."
"Hey! They can't touch us with tonjotonjo wood from Black Forest."
[audience Here's a hunting horn (imitating) tulo tulo tulo tulo --
You hear the Muhyoza.]
I heard someone say, "Yo yo yo yo He's hit you!"
He asked, "What's he hit me with?"
"Tonjotonjo wood from Black Forest."
He said, "Let's retreat, child,
See, your brother lies fallen in lugunga grass."
He said, "Didn't I tell you so? They've surrounded them."
Iyo-o they crossed over.
When they'd crossed at Kyenyabasa,
They climbed up at Muhutwe.
He said, "That village there, have them cut it to the ground."
I saw them cut the village to the ground.
They said, "That way is too far, Bahyoza."
When they came to Ibwela, I saw they cut, and the earth reddened.
At Slaughter Mountain they cut, and the earth reddened.
And they followed on to Kabilizi, and it reddened.
In Ikunyu I saw...
Listen!
(audience Bespeak yourself, recite your praise--)
Self Praise Recitation Begins
I speak to you, I chant my praise, I am Kataishi.
Travelling, I am Never-climbed-upon.
I am The-Abrasive-one.
Yes indeed, you chant your praise, you chant your praise.
A Muhyoza chants his praise and brings it out from inside.
He straightened his back, the Child-of-the-Angry-one, the Muhyoza.
He said, 'Chant your praise - Let me hear what you say.'
I leave off."
Another Self Praise Recitation Begins
He said, " I am Banana-stumps, Child of Afflictions.
I-am-compelled, Bad-dream of the Causer-of-Retreats.
An Ancient feud has fixed a scar upon my eye.
I am of Bucha and the Decider-of-cases
A child of a Muyego wife, I come from Long-necked Giraffe.
I come from Katoma of Mileego
Where warriors purchase cattle.
I am of the Father of Rocky-outcroppings,
Kahima Malima of Igogo in Kigalama of Ntahi.
A large and beautiful forest.
I come from Wet-one, Things-overhanging. I am ruled by Kagege forest.
Let me tell you so you'll know I am the child of warriors.
I am the child of Amri Binamungu
Whom they call Ta Butono.
I am the child of Munabi Gusitini.
I praise where I was born!
Father and mother.
Those two. Mother did not come with a latent fetus in her belly.
I was born in Kitendagulo of Nyawela.
In a small village that has no stones.
Miziku bush I come from nkeke grass.
I was cut from It-will-not-set.
I am a child of the Path-through-swamps.
Good watering places I tell you.
I come from Kilombe.
Kanyamuli
Of Lukoko river.
Kakindo village
I am child of Kyanyalwela
I am not one who hides himself .
Conquer Provider.
Self-Praise Poetry ends
He did not ask another word.
On Slaughter Mountain
I saw they cut and the earth reddened.
In Makongola
They climbed to Bushaka
And they cut and the earth reddened.
In Bushagala and Kamachumu
And they cut and the earth reddened.
When he looked toward Kamachumu he saw into Lwanda
And into Busingo.
He saw into Lutenge.
He said, "Go cut that village to the ground.
From there men came who killed my own men."
I saw At Busingo they cut and the earth reddened.
And Lwanda they cut, and the earth reddened.
Lutenge-e
I heard they cut, and the earth reddened.
When it reddened he stood at Lutenge and crowed.
He looked at Buganguzi, there at Nkungu's home.
He said, "Which village is that?"
They said, "It is Buganguzi, Nkungu's home."
He said, "From there come Banyaihangilo,
Killers of my men."
I heard them speak, the royal drums.
They spoke without cease.
I heard when men came rolling
Up a hill,
The Muhyoza, Sling-carrier
They shot him with an arrow in the knee.
All of them, Banyaihangilo of Mbatama village,
When the arrows began to fly among the leaves,
You'd think that children played at shooting reeds.
He who kneels to let his arrow fly
Finds Bahyoza split his crown.
They did not ask another word.
They said, "The little ones that run,"
They said, "we'll chop in two."
They went swiftly. It is they in Nshamba.
And there they cut, and the earth reddened.
In Ihangilo there
They say, "Let us respect the palace."
I heard the Munyaihangilo,
King Ntale say, "Child
That Kabale king is a bad one.
He is the very lion of my name,
An Ancient Boar, Makes-Hunters-Wary.
Child of Igalami and Bashaija,
Raiser-of-Howls in the early morning.
Great Man-climbing leopard of Kahigi,
Omwilu of Kainyombo and Nyaimoma,
(aside that cigarette is bothering me)
Calves that do not hinder, Thighs that do not shame. "
He said, "Go and pay tribute to him.
The royal women say, "Our Lord!
Let's go and have a look. A Hima who kills Hinda?"
The Hinda women put on their leather aprons.
Himself had already become an old man.
The tip of his such-and-such had become a lighter red.
What eyes they fell upon him with!
"He-e" they said, "And the Hinda are fearful. The Hinda are fearful.
This old man?" They said, "Eh-eh-eh my dears!
One to be feared? Never!
Don't say such a thing, my lord."
Himself, he did not say, "I give respect to the King of Ihangilo."
When he arrived at Kashekela village,
He said, "Build here, construct."
They built, they constructed.
They paid tribute, they paid tribute.
Cattle came that day.
He said, "Slaughter these for Bahyoza to eat."
They brought others saying, "These are for your men."
They said," The king did not send Tse-tse-fly.
He has already gone by the road to Zinzaland."
He did not ask another word.
He said, "I'll till the earth and stay six months."
He said, "I'll see if he regains his strength
And frees his men to fight.
Rage I have much.
I might rise up and go attack the king of Zinzaland,
And of Kalagwe of Nono,
And of Rwanda of Mibale."
They said, "Accept settlement, O king.
Do not set kingdoms trading blows."
They built, they dwelled, they protected planted millet.
They harvest it and cook it into porridge.
He said, "Go and tell King Ntale,
Say, 'Do nothing else.
On this day he returns home.'"
Self Praise Recitation Begins
I am She-who-feels-another's-pain of Makes-certain.
Thunder said, 'I'll strike you!
I said, 'You strike me? I am the Grower-of-sharpened-points,
Iron hammers like a spirit medium's.
I come from Kibale of Katwenge.
I am of Iron things.
I am of two genitalia.
I come from Katembe and Mulongo.
I am of Igombe village. I am of Mankala-player. I am of Kagolo.
I am of Conqueror of Little-guardian, Old-wrinkled-one of the Bulls.
I came from Wanderer's home.
I am a child of the Balwani clan
Whose prohibition is black-spot cattle.
Their prohibition is not like other people's.
Their prohibition is not minnows.
Their prohibition is not this or that. Their prohibition is an entire cow.
And that's my prohibition too.
Hail, Father-creator, mount raids. We return home.
Self-Praise Poetry ends
Tse-tse-fly pecked up what would kill him.
It's true, the one to die pecks up what kills it.
(You've played it! --)
Change in Melody
Push on,
Push on, children.
Push homeward Flows-over-boundaries,
And Kabushumba Itimba,
Push on, Munyaninga, Giant Spear of Nyababa.
